title: Gold falls on stronger dollar amid renewed US-Iran tensions
summary: By Noel John April 20 (Reuters) - Gold prices fell on Monday as the dollar firmed, while news the Strait of Hormuz is closed again pushed oil prices higher and revived inflation fears.

title: Oil prices and stocks climb as the US-Iran standoff keeps the Strait of Hormuz in limbo
summary: Oil prices climbed more than 5% while Asian shares also advanced Monday as a standoff between Iran and the U.S. prevented tankers from using the Strait of Hormuz.  The Persian Gulf waterway was closed again after Iran reversed a decision to reopen the strait and President Donald Trump said a U.S. Navy blockade of Iranian ports remains in effect.  U.S. benchmark crude gained 5.6% to $87.20 a barrel, while Brent crude, the international standard, was up 5.3% at $95.16 a barrel.

title: Stocks Are Back at Records, but Bond Investors Haven’t Joined the Party
summary: A disconnect is emerging on Wall Street: Stock investors are already celebrating as though the Iran war never happened, but other markets are telling a more cautious story.  While the S&P 500 and Nasdaq composite indexes are both at all-time highs, worries about lasting damage to Middle East energy infrastructure have kept longer-dated oil futures well above their prewar levels.  Treasury yields also remain elevated, with investors concerned that higher inflation will make it harder for the Federal Reserve to cut interest rates.

title: Stock Futures Are Falling and Oil Jumps Higher Ahead of New Iran Talks
summary: U.S. stock and oil futures are poised to begin trading later on Sunday after President Donald Trump’s latest threats against Iran and as U.S. officials head to Pakistan on Monday for another round of talks on Tuesday.  Shortly after 6 p.m. Eastern time on Sunday, Dow Jones Industrial Average futures were down 450 points, or about 0.9%, while S&P 500 futures were down 0.8% as were Nasdaq 100 futures.  Brent crude continuous contract prices jumped 6.8% to $96.58 a barrel on Sunday, while WTI continuous contract prices rose 7.2% to $88.50 a barrel.

title: Oil Prices Jump Despite Trump’s Call for Peace Talks
summary: Oil prices opened sharply higher Sunday evening in New York to climb back near $90 a barrel following two days of confusion over whether the Strait of Hormuz is actually open to shipping traffic and President Trump’s call for a new round of peace talks this week with Iran.  U.S. oil futures for May delivery dropped more than 11% Friday to settle at $83.85 a barrel after Iran’s foreign minister declared free passage through the waterway that is vital to global energy markets.  This weekend, however, Iran’s military denied that passage was safe.
